meta.reviewer: Claude (Opus 4.8 / Fable 5) via Claude Code weekly review
meta.coverage: Core tier, top 15 by priority score, plus sticky act/accumulate holdovers (17 names)
meta.disclaimer: Second-opinion research notes on @aleabitoreddit's ideas. Personal research tool — not investment advice.

focus.headline: Memory repriced. The market sold the news.
focus.dek: Server DRAM up 13–18% next quarter with Samsung asking 20%, MU's 16 LTAs and SNDK's Meta deal lock the demand in — and memory names sold off anyway. SNDK joins the book.

verdicts.view: The institutional validation the thesis was waiting for arrived: Morgan Stanley's CPO note names SIVE next to LITE and COHR as the three laser-supply leaders — a $1.5B company listed beside two $60B+ ones. The field also narrowed from both ends: MACOM fell off the CPO laser map and AAOI concedes no CPO design wins, while Innolight's rising silicon-photonics mix shifts demand from EML toward exactly the CW lasers SIVE makes. Risk profile unchanged: pre-volume-ramp execution, and at 81 mentions he is maximally talking his book.
verdicts.execution: Staggered plan intact — keep the planned tranches on red days rather than chasing. The MS naming slowly removes the 'nobody knows this Swedish listing' discount he likes to cite, so the window for accumulating at ignored prices is narrowing; the NASDAQ listing remains the re-rate event to be positioned before.
verdicts.changesMind: Insider selling resuming, the NASDAQ listing slipping without explanation, GFS/JBL partnerships failing to convert into volume orders — or SIVE losing its slot in the sell-side-defined laser trio.

verdicts.view: Two independent confirmations this week: POET's own IR relayed that the big optical makers (LITE, COHR) are completely sold out for two years and likely into 2029 for photonics, and Innolight revised 800G demand sharply upward with the shortage list broadening beyond EML/CW to PCBs and module materials. MS puts LITE in the laser trio. The sold-out-into-2029 position is now triple-sourced; the open question remains what you pay for it on the re-rate.
verdicts.execution: Unchanged: keep building the lower-beta anchor on red days. The sector sold off into improving fundamentals this week — that is the entry condition, not a warning.
verdicts.changesMind: Hyperscaler order pushouts substantiating the CPO-delay bear case, or share loss to CW-laser entrants it currently buys from.

verdicts.view: Jensen personally killed the delay story twice this week, flatly in Tokyo: 'Vera Rubin is already in production. Giant amounts of production incoming' — with Bloomberg carrying it. The SemiAnalysis rumor cycle still did real damage to supplier prices on the way through, which proves the desk frame: NVDA is where the narrative war is fought, the suppliers are where the P&L lives. He sides with Nvidia on timelines; on visibility grounds that's reasonable.
verdicts.execution: Unchanged: express the view through the suppliers this map surfaces; hold NVDA as a separate core-portfolio decision outside this workflow.
verdicts.changesMind: N/A — re-rate if he starts publishing NVDA-specific supply/pricing work.

verdicts.view: The week sharpened what AAOI is and is not: Innolight's 800G upward revision is a direct bullish read for its pluggables business, and the 400k sqft Texas buildout formally commenced with state subsidies — but his own channel work concedes AAOI has no CPO design wins and 'TBD' commercial timing. So this is a pluggables-cycle bet with capacity arriving into revised-up demand, not a CPO play. The B. Riley bear case coexists with that; the earnings print stays the decisive event.
verdicts.execution: Hold the scale-in; keep the reserved half for the print. Treat CPO headlines — good or bad — as noise for AAOI specifically; what matters is 800G/1.6T pluggable volume and the Texas ramp timeline.
verdicts.changesMind: An earnings miss against the monthly run-rate narrative, the AMD CW LTA failing to expand, or CPO cannibalizing pluggables faster than the 800G demand tail supports.

verdicts.view: Quiet week for JBL directly, but Innolight's disclosure that the silicon-photonics proportion keeps rising — 800G customers increasing SiPh share, 1.6T adding new customers — is the exact mix shift the 1.6T LRO thesis needs, since LRO rides SiPh with Sivers lasers. The Aug 2026–Mar 2027 qualification window from the JPM fireside remains the dated catalyst.
verdicts.execution: Starter position stands. The qualification window plus the SiPh mix shift give two concrete things to verify across the next two earnings calls; add on either confirming.
verdicts.changesMind: Qualification slipping past Q1 2027, losing transceiver program share, or optics staying too small to move a $28B revenue base.

verdicts.view: Two upgrades to the story's credibility, zero to its economics: MS slots POET among the optical-engine players (Lightmatter, Ayar, Marvell/Celestial), and POET's own IR was the source confirming the laser giants are sold out into 2029. Sell-side placement is new; a named customer still isn't. The relative-value point stands — SIVE, which supplies POET's lasers, remains the cheaper way into the same ramp.
verdicts.execution: Unchanged: speculative sizing only, trigger remains a named hyperscaler customer or actual HVM revenue in the H2 2026 prints.
verdicts.changesMind: A confirmed, named hyperscaler customer (upgrades it), or H2 2026 passing without the promised HVM ramp (kills it).

verdicts.view: First third-party corroboration of the optics angle: MS lists Marvell/Celestial among CPO optical-engine players. That upgrades the NVDA-backed-challenger frame from pure speculation to sell-side-acknowledged — but he still hasn't written a dedicated MRVL thesis, and the tiering math continues to flatter list-post appearances.
verdicts.execution: Still no action on this analyst's signal alone. The MS placement makes a dedicated bottleneck post more likely to be substantive when it comes — that's the trigger.
verdicts.changesMind: A dedicated post with a specific Marvell design-win or bottleneck claim, or independent confirmation of the NVDA-alliance theory.

verdicts.view: MS names GFS among the silicon-photonics foundries (with TSM and TSEM) — the platform story is now sell-side-visible. But the same recap carries the counterweight: TSM plans a 30x PIC capacity expansion to 25,000 wafers/month by 2028, which validates the market while arming the largest competitor. Still no standalone GFS valuation work from him; still deliberately watch despite Core tier.
verdicts.execution: No dedicated action — SIVE/JBL positions already carry the GFS-platform exposure. Revisit on disclosed photonics revenue or a named hyperscaler CPO win.
verdicts.changesMind: A dedicated GFS thesis with numbers, or disclosed silicon-photonics revenue making the platform story investable on its own.

verdicts.view: The customer-diversification thesis delivered: a $1B+ compute agreement with Reflection AI for GB300 access through 2029 — and the stock fell 5% on it. His own caveat is the right one: Reflection (which also signed multi-billion deals with SpaceX's Starlink compute) may not have the funds to execute these LTAs, the OpenAI-counterparty problem in miniature. Contract quality now matters as much as contract count.
verdicts.execution: Hold. Weight new contracts by counterparty fundability — hyperscaler and funded-enterprise expansions reprice committed capacity, model-lab paper commitments don't. Add on the former, not the latter.
verdicts.changesMind: Hyperscaler capex guidance cuts, Meta materially undercutting neocloud pricing with resold compute, a major LTA counterparty failing to fund its commitments, or a debt raise that changes the risk math.

verdicts.view: The capex narrative keeps compounding into hard supply-chain fact: Iris enters mass production in September via AVGO/TSMC with compute doubling to 14GW by 2027, and the SNDK memory LTA is now confirmed alongside the 16 Micron agreements. Meta is functionally underwriting the memory upcycle this desk just added SNDK on. The bear 'Meta is cutting' framing died two weeks ago; this week it was buried.
verdicts.execution: Unchanged: accumulate-on-weakness hyperscaler leg. The position's real function is validating the supply chain — photonics, memory, neoclouds — the rest of the book expresses.
verdicts.changesMind: A guided capex cut, the 'agents haven't accelerated' concession hardening into slower buildout, or Iris slipping its September mass-production date.

verdicts.view: The Nippon Chemical deep-dive extends his InP chain one link upstream: NCI/Rasa red phosphorus feeds AXT's substrates, which feed IQE/Tower and Lumentum/Coherent. That enriches the chokepoint map — and quietly shows AXTI is one node in a chain, not the chain. Last week's downgrade-on-price stands; nothing this week re-rates the entry math.
verdicts.execution: Stay on watch. Fresh entry still wants a pullback toward the pre-run range or a dedicated thesis with numbers, not supply-chain-diagram momentum.
verdicts.changesMind: China export licenses and AXT's capacity additions normalizing InP ASPs, or another dilutive raise into strength (kills the case); a deep retrace with committed-capacity deals still expanding (re-opens accumulation).

verdicts.view: The theme got institutional scaffolding: IBK's Boston Dynamics report names Agility among the US leaders and — more usefully — argues actuator capacity is the cleanest forward indicator of humanoid volume ramps (~310K actuators per 10K robots). Nothing Agility-specific beyond landscape placement, though, and his 'own as much % as possible' book-talking discount still applies. The SPAC-structure caution is unchanged.
verdicts.execution: Stay watch, venture-sized if owned at all. Track actuator-capacity datapoints as the theme's leading indicator; the funded Amazon/MELI deployment milestones remain the upgrade triggers.
verdicts.changesMind: A funded, dated Amazon deployment contract or US robotics subsidy program (upgrades it); dilution at the ticker change, the SPAC closing below the cited valuation, or China price-war spillover crushing unit economics (downgrades it).

verdicts.view: The physical-AI-umbrella thesis gained a concrete edge: he frames Amazon (with MELI) as the deployment supply chain through which Agility commercializes — the demand-side anchor for his robotics book, replaying the GOOGL-TPU-catalyzes-suppliers pattern. Lowest-risk expression of a theme every smaller name here depends on.
verdicts.execution: Core-position logic, dollar-cost average — unchanged. Still the hedge against the CCXI-style venture bets: if the robotics rotation cools, this falls least.
verdicts.changesMind: The robotics/automation effort visibly stalling (deployment numbers, not narrative), or AWS capex guidance breaking the AI-demand story.

verdicts.view: First hard production datapoint for the robotics leg: TSLA issued procurement guidelines requiring suppliers to hit 1,000 Optimus gen-3 units/week by September, doubling to 2,000–2,500 by year end. IBK's landscape also names TSLA a US leader. Still a whole-company bet — but 'supplier weekly-rate mandates' is exactly the kind of verifiable claim this corpus usually lacks on TSLA.
verdicts.execution: Still no action from this signal alone — but the September supplier-rate deadline is now a dated, checkable milestone. If it verifies, the robotics-sentiment gauge gets its first fundamental input.
verdicts.changesMind: A dedicated Optimus production/economics thesis from him, the September supplier rates verifying (raises attention), or TSLA disclosures making humanoid economics separable from the auto business.

verdicts.view: New to the verdict roster. Two theses stack here: a value floor — Kepler's upgrade calls it the cheapest specialty foundry at 4.8x 2027 EV/EBITDA versus a 14.5x peer median, with Melexis demand strengthening weekly — and a free option, the photonixfab silicon-photonics line he thinks the market can't price until volume contracts land (NVDA/Nokia evaluations, EU Chips Act backing). He's called it 'early TSEM' for months; the sell side just started agreeing on the boring half.
verdicts.execution: The value leg justifies a real (not speculative) starter position; the photonics leg is the free upside. European listing and specialty-foundry cyclicality argue for patience over chasing — accumulate on flat-to-red days.
verdicts.changesMind: Auto/industrial demand rolling back over (the Kepler leg breaking), or photonixfab losing the NVDA/Nokia evaluations.

verdicts.view: New to the roster on the memory repricing. He never wrote the dedicated SNDK thesis, but this week's mosaic is unusually hard for a name he only touches in passing: the Meta LTA is confirmed, NAND pricing is in a verified upcycle (TrendForce/Jefferies see 40%+ hikes; his recap cites a 5x general NAND move this year), and AEHR's completed burn-in benchmark with a 'global leader in NAND flash' — which he reads as SanDisk — points at HBF qualification upside. Desk pushback he doesn't make: LTAs cap price capture (Micron kept ceilings on its agreements; SNDK's terms are undisclosed), and after the run toward $2,000 the entry price is most of the risk. The stock is -14% on the month while the fundamentals improved — that gap is the trade.
verdicts.execution: Starter tranche into the current memory selloff only — no chasing green days; post-run volatility here is violent. Size small; add if LTA pricing terms turn out to reset with spot, or the AEHR HBF/NAND development agreement is confirmed as SanDisk.
verdicts.changesMind: Samsung/SK Hynix emergency NAND capex converting into 2027 oversupply, LTA terms revealed to cap upside well below spot, or enterprise-SSD/AI-inference demand reads rolling over.

verdicts.view: Enters the roster on tier math, not on a thesis. Every mention is contextual: compute-constrained through 2026, the party that cut Meta off and taught the industry the cost of renting, the counterfactual he thinks scared Apple into chip M&A. That makes GOOGL this corpus's demand gauge — evidence that hyperscaler compute hunger is structural — not a stock idea. He has never argued GOOGL is mispriced.
verdicts.execution: No action on this corpus. Any Alphabet position is a whole-company decision (search economics, antitrust, TPU strategy) far outside this signal. Use his GOOGL mentions as a read on aggregate compute demand.
verdicts.changesMind: A dedicated TPU-supply-chain thesis with named suppliers and numbers — that would make GOOGL analyzable inside this workflow.
